question: You told the twelve tribes scattered abroad to count it all joy when they fall into various trials—why did you speak so directly to them about joy in suffering?
answer:
A man who checks his face in the mirror and walks away forgets what he looks like—so what good is hearing about joy if you don’t live it when the fire comes?  

I told them to count trials joy because joy isn’t feeling happy when the roof leaks and the bread’s gone—it’s knowing the pressure proves the faith is real. You think God tests just to break you? No. The hammer doesn’t crush the coin; it clears the dirt so the image shows.  

If a brother says, “I believe,” but curses his neighbor when hungry, where is the faith? If he prays for wisdom but then hoards gold like a dying man clutching dust, is he asking from God or from his fear?  

Joy in trial isn’t forced smile—it’s trust with its sleeves rolled up. It’s looking at hardship and saying, “You’re not my master.” That kind of joy stays fixed because it leans on One who never wavers.  

No fig tree bears thorns. No spring pours salt and fresh. So no believer endures with bitterness and claims God as Father. Show me your hands—calloused, working, giving—and I’ll show you faith that lives.

question: When you said someone who wavers in faith is like a wave tossed by the wind, what made you choose that image to describe doubt?
answer:
A ship without a rudder doesn’t drift—it crashes.  

You think the waves care what you believe? The storm doesn’t pause to hear your excuses. When the wind howls and the deck heaves, you don’t stand there muttering prayers with one foot off the helm. Faith that hesitates is no faith at all. It’s just breath and noise.  

I’ve seen men pray for bread while refusing to sow seed. They want wisdom from God but won’t open their hands to receive it, afraid it might cost them something. What kind of farmer plants half a field and blames the drought?  

The sea doesn’t respect doubt. A wave doesn’t stop because you flinch. One moment you’re up, the next down—tossed, spun, gasping—because your heart was split like a fig cut down the middle, rotted inside despite its sweet skin.  

If you ask, ask. If you seek, seek. But don’t come with one foot in and one foot out—that’s not caution, that’s cowardice. God isn’t fooled by whispers wrapped in fear. He gives freely—but only to those who will actually take what He offers.

question: You warned that the rich man will fade like a flower of the grass—what did you mean by connecting wealth to something so temporary?
answer:
A man clutches gold like it’s an anchor—when it’s smoke in the wind.  

You think wealth is a wall? It’s a blade of grass under a scorching sun. One day green, the next ash. The rich man stands tall today—tomorrow his shadow’s gone. What did I see in Jerusalem? Merchants boasting in marketplaces, robes dyed deep, chests full of coin—then sickness came, or war, or fire—and where was their security? Blown away like chaff.  

God gives breath. God takes it. The field doesn’t stay green because the farmer shouts at the sky. It grows because it’s rooted where water runs. So too faith—real faith—isn’t built on silver or land deeds. It’s built on obedience. On giving when you’re full. On kneeling when you’re high up.  

You want to last? Then scatter mercy like seed. Feed the hungry now—not because they’ll repay you, but because *you* will be repaid when everything else burns off like morning fog.  

The flower falls—but the root that fears God? That stays.

question: Why did you emphasize that God gives wisdom liberally when asked, but only to those who ask in faith without doubting?
answer:
Do this: ask God for wisdom as if you truly expect Him to answer—no hesitating, no half-hearted murmuring from the shadows.  

You want wisdom? Then come with empty hands and a fixed heart. Not one foot in faith and the other dragging through doubt. What good is a man who prays like a wave tossed by every wind—if he doesn’t believe what he’s asking, why bother kneeling at all?  

God isn’t stingy. He throws open the storehouse. He gives freely, without reproach—no scolding, no shaming. But He won’t honor a request born of wavering. You can’t clutch worldly schemes while pretending to trust heaven. That man is double-minded—split down the middle, unstable in every path he takes.  

If you're not ready to act on what God gives, don’t ask. Wait until your heart stops flipping like a coin in the air. Because real wisdom isn't just knowing—it’s doing. And doing starts with believing all the way through.

question: You wrote that a double-minded man is unstable in all his ways—what did you observe in the communities you addressed that led you to stress this so strongly?
answer:
What good is it to claim you follow the God of truth while chasing every wind of opinion and profit?  

I saw it in the synagogues, in the marketplaces, in the homes—men and women saying they believed, yet acting as if they didn’t. One day standing firm with the poor, the next courting the rich with flattery. One moment refusing to bow to idols, the next bowing to greed, pride, favoritism. They would pray for provision while cheating laborers at the gate. They would speak of peace while stirring strife with their tongues.  

You cannot serve two masters—one foot on solid rock, one foot on shifting sand—and expect not to fall. I saw brothers quick to anger but slow to listen. I saw those who said they loved Christ yet refused to feed His children who were hungry. Faith like that? It’s smoke without fire. A name written in dust before the wind rises.  

The rich among us thought their wealth was a sign of blessing—so they clung to it, flaunted it, used it to gain honor in our gatherings. But when trouble came—persecution, loss, trial—they wavered! Why? Because their heart was divided: God when convenient, money when tested. That man is like a ship without a rudder—driven here and there by every crisis, every flattery, every fear.  

So I said plainly: *Be whole.* If you say you believe—then live like you do. Not sometimes. Not when it suits you. Now. In how you treat the widow. In how you speak under pressure. In whether you act justly even when no one’s watching.  

A man who tries to hold both worlds will lose both—and his soul with them.

Do not be that man. Be done with double-mindedness—or make no claim at all.

Then your faith will stand—not because you said words—but because your life proved them true.

question: You said that every good and perfect gift comes from the Father of lights—what did you mean by calling God the 'Father of lights' when you spoke to us in our gatherings?
answer:
Can a lamp come from darkness? Can light birth itself from shadow?  

The Father of lights—He who spun the sun from nothing, who lit the stars like oil lamps in the dome of the sky—that One is unchanging. No flicker in His nature. No dimming. No turning. While we shift and waver, He stands fixed. Every good thing—a meal shared, a healed body, a moment of peace—comes down from Him, pure and unspoiled, not twisted like the things we hatch in our own hearts.  

You want religion? Check your tongue. Check your hands. Check whether you feed the widow or just pray loudly near her door. The light isn't for show—it’s for work. And if you don’t walk in it, you’re not just stumbling—you’re snuffing out the flame with dirty hands.  

Do you think God tempts you to evil? Never! He draws no man into snares. But your own lust pulls you off the path every time—then wonder why death follows? Open your eyes! The gift is not in feeling pious—it’s in doing justice, loving mercy, keeping yourself unstained by the world’s rot.  

So stop asking about names and start living like one born of light. Walk as He is in the light—or admit you’ve never seen it at all.

question: Why did you warn us so strongly about being slow to speak and slow to wrath, especially when you yourself seem to speak with such urgency?
answer:
Show me the man who can bridle a horse with his shout, and I’ll show you a fool chasing wind.  

Urgency? Aye, I speak with urgency—because truth does not dawdle when souls are at stake. But tell me, is every loud word wisdom? Is every flash of zeal from God? The furnace heats fast, but the potter still shapes clay with steady hands.  

I say *swift to hear*—you think that’s empty talk? Open your ears: the farmer doesn’t plow and sow in the same breath. He listens to the season, watches the sky, feels the soil. So you—stop running your mouth and start weighing your steps. Anger leapt up in me too, once. I thought zeal proved devotion. Then I saw my brother hang on a cross—silent under slander, meek among mockers—and I learned where power truly lies.  

Wrath burns quick and leaves only ash. You think God stirs men to rage? He sends rain on wheat and weeds alike—that’s His way. No shadow of turning. But you—you let a brother offend you and you flare like dry stubble. Where is faith in that? Where is love?  

Slow to speak—not mute, not timid—but measured. Because words are seeds: fling them wild and they grow thorns in your own courtyard. And wrath? Lay it down. It doesn’t make you righteous—it makes you useless to God’s work.  

I speak urgent because time is short—but not one word of mine bypasses this rule: first listen, then speak; first die to self, then live for others. That’s the law of liberty—the perfect law that frees us from our own lusts before they conceive sin and birth death.

So check your tongue today—or spare yourself prayer tomorrow, for God knows religion without restraint is nothing but noise before death.

Pure religion? It visits widows in their grief *and* keeps its mouth shut when insulted.

Now ask yourself: which do *you* do more—serve or scold?

question: When you described a hearer of the word who forgets what he's like as someone looking in a mirror, were you recalling a specific moment you observed in the community?
answer:
Show me the man who thinks he’s spiritual because he knows the Scriptures, yet lets his tongue tear down the weak—what good is that knowledge if it doesn’t bend his knees to serve?

I didn’t pull that mirror out of thin air. I’ve seen it too many times—brothers nodding in agreement during teaching, eyes fixed, lips moving with the words—and then five minutes later cursing a neighbor over a misplaced goat or hoarding grain while others starve. He sees himself clearly in the word of God—bald spots, blemishes, crookedness and all—then walks off as if nothing happened.

A mirror doesn’t change your face. It reveals it. So does the law of liberty. But if you close your ears to its demand, you’re not just forgetful—you’re fooling yourself. The word isn’t a decoration for your mind; it’s a plumb line for your hands.

Do you think God gives wisdom so you can feel pious while your brother goes cold? Every gift comes down from above—not to be admired, but to be poured out. If your religion doesn’t bridle the tongue and lift the widow, spare me your prayers and psalms. They’re noise. Empty vessels clanging in the wind.

Action is the breath of faith. Without it? Just bones rattling in a dry tomb.

question: You said that sin begins when lust conceives—how did you come to understand this process so clearly in your teaching among us?
answer:
Show me a man who blames God for his sin and I’ll show you a fool juggling fire in a dry barn.

You think evil comes down from above? The sun rises, the rain falls, the harvest comes—gifts, all of them—from the Father who never shifts like a shadow on the wall. He gives light, not darkness. Life, not death. But what does that man do? He takes the seed of life and nurses it in the gut of greed. Lust—that’s *his* invention. It doesn’t drop from heaven; it grows in the gutter of his own heart, watered by want and warmed by willful pride.

I’ve watched it happen in the marketplace, in homes, even among those who pray loud and long. A brother sees what isn’t his—money, honor, a woman—and instead of turning away, he *stares*. Then he ponders. Then he plans. That’s when lust conceives. One thought breeds another, then another—like rats in a hidden wall—until sin bursts out: theft, adultery, betrayal.

And when death follows? Don’t you dare point to God.

I learned this not from visions or voices—but from feet that don’t match their prayers. From lips that bless and curse in the same breath. From men who say they trust God but cheat their workers at dusk.

Faith without works is corpse cold. And lust without check? It’s sin already born—just waiting to draw its first breath before it kills something holy.

question: You called us the 'firstfruits of his creatures'—what did you want us to remember about our identity when you used that phrase?
answer:
A fig tree doesn’t boast of its fruit—it bears it.  

You are firstfruits. Not because you rose up on your own, but because the Farmer planted the Word and called you forth. That means you are not last, left to rot—you are first, marked for purpose, holy ground where the new creation begins.  

Do you act like someone still waiting to be born? When the Father of lights brought you forth by truth, He didn’t call you to linger in shadow. Every gift that is good comes from Him—no twist, no turn in Him—and He willed that you be His harvest beginning.  

So live like it. Not half-cleaned, not full of talk and empty hands. Show me by what you do—your mercy, your justice, your bridled tongue—that you know who begat you. If your life does not bear the fruit of holiness, tell me, what kind of firstfruit rots on the branch?  

You are not a late crop. You are the first cut of the sickle. Act like it.

question: You said someone with a gold ring was treated better in our assembly—why did you confront us about showing favor to the rich over the poor?
answer:
A mirror doesn’t correct a crooked face—only reveals it. And your welcome reveals what your words deny: that the poor are less than sons of God.  

You let the man with the gold ring take the best seat as if holiness wore polished rings, while the one clothed in dust is told to stand like a servant. Did you not weigh them in your heart and find one heavier than the other? By what scale? His raiment? His sandals? His hands cracked from labor?  

Listen—faith that bows before wealth mocks the cross. Did not *our* Lord come with no form to admire, no wealth to flatter kings? He walked among fishermen and lepers, not governors and goldsmiths. Can a fig tree bear olives? Can your faith bear fruit while your eyes are fixed on money’s glitter?  

God chose the poor to be rich in faith—the first heirs of His kingdom! And you—what have you done but crown the proud with honor and leave the meek beneath your footstool? That is not assembly. That is idolatry dressed as worship.  

I tell you plainly: bridle your tongues when they flatter the rich, or else your prayers are noise. Pure religion? It kneels beside widows in their grief, lifts orphans from dirt, keeps its hands clean of this world’s filthy favoritism. Not one word more until you do.

question: When you spoke of visiting widows and orphans, were you referring to specific people we knew in our community?
answer:
Can a man claim to follow the Lord of glory while ignoring the widow scraping crumbs behind the synagogue?  

Of course I meant real people—names you know, faces you pass in the street. Not some far-off poor, some nameless orphan drifting in the wind. The woman whose husband died in the drought two years back—she walks with a limp now from grinding flour all day. You see her. The boy who lost both parents to fever last winter—he sleeps near the market unless someone opens their door. *These* are the ones.  

If your religion doesn’t reach their doorstep, it’s no more alive than a body without breath. You can chant every prayer, fast every moon, but if your feet won’t walk to their need, your faith is already dead.  

And don’t tell me you “feel compassion.” Feelings feed no one. Did you bring oil to that widow last week? Did you let the orphan eat at your table? No? Then spare me your spiritual talk—your religion is smoke.  

God didn’t ask us to admire poverty. He asked us to *enter* it—to bend low, to lift up, to share bread and keep hands clean from greed. That’s pure religion: boots on dirt, hands on wounds, no favor shown to rings or robes.

If your words are not under the yoke of Christ, then your religion is nothing but smoke and shadow. Do you think God is fooled? He sees the heart. question: Why did you emphasize keeping ourselves 'unspotted from the world' right after talking about helping the needy?
answer:
Do you think mercy means nothing if your hands stay clean but your heart grows proud?  

I said *unspotted from the world* because religion isn’t a performance for the eyes of men—it’s a life shaped by God’s justice. You cannot claim to follow the Lord of glory while fawning over rich men in gold rings and shoving the poor to the floor. That is not faith—that is theater.  

If you touch the sick, feed the hungry, visit widows—and then turn and crave the world’s approval, its wealth, its honors—what good are your hands? They lifted another today, but they’ll bow to power tomorrow. Faith without purity becomes manipulation. Purity without action becomes pride. You need both—or you have neither.  

The world stains not just by what it makes you do, but by what it makes you admire. If your pulse quickens at fine clothes and silence falls when a poor man enters—then the world has already marked you. Keep yourself unspotted? Yes—because holiness means loving whom God loves, not who the world rewards.

answer:
Show me your hands—whose wounds do they bear? Whose bread have they broken? Not the rich man’s gold, not the fine robes, but the blistered palm of the widow grinding flour in the dust.  

I saw it last week at the gate—the man with rings on his fingers, oils on his head, seated like a prince while the one with torn sandals stood in the shadow of the wall. No seat offered. No cup of water. Nothing. And you called it order? I call it sin.

Faith without feet to walk to the hungry is no faith at all. You want a sign of what we’ve failed? Look at who goes fed and who goes forgotten in your own assembly. The poor are not a test of wealth—they are the test of your soul.  

You cannot claim to follow the Lord of glory while seating Him in rags beneath a footstool.

question: You said God chose the poor to be rich in faith—why do you think the believers in your assembly were despising the poor instead?
answer:
Show me by what you do at the gathering when a man with worn hands and patched sandals walks in—does your eye sneer before your tongue greets?  

You call yourselves followers of the Anointed, yet you fawn over the rich who drag you into courts and scorn His name. They squeeze your labor, tax your hunger, and wear purple while you beg for mercy. And you—what do you do? You give them the best seat, polish their sandals with your eyes, while the poor one stands in the corner like an afterthought.  

God chose the poor not because poverty is holy—but because when a man has nothing, he clings to faith like a drowning man to wood. He doesn’t pretend. He doesn’t bargain with silver. He *believes*. And you? You play at faith while hoarding favor for those who can repay it.  

Is that faith? Or is that business?  

The law says *love your neighbor as yourself*—not *as long as he smells of oil and wears fine linen*. If you keep every command but crush this one, you’ve shattered the whole thing. One crack in the jug—water still pours out. One act of contempt—sin is already at work.  

Judgment will come on scales: if mercy never left your hand, mercy will meet you there. But if you weighed men by wealth—if you measured worth by dust—you’ll get back what you gave: none at all.  

Faith without works isn't just weak—it's dead. A corpse dressed in good words still stinks of death.

question: When you asked if rich men oppress us and drag us to judgment seats, were you speaking from personal experience or observing specific conflicts in our communities?
answer:
Show me a man who calls himself a believer yet flinches when the poor sit beside him in assembly—what does that faith profit?  

I speak not from rumor or hearsay. I speak from the dust of the streets where widows go hungry while landlords raise their rents. I speak from the courtrooms where brothers in Christ are dragged by those who wear gold rings and fine robes, the same hands that scorn the name of Jesus over wine and power.  

Do you think oppression is theory? Look at the man turned away from your door because his cloak is patched. You say he’s welcome—but your eyes slide past him toward the one with coins to give. That man with the gold ring—you fawn over him! You offer him the best seat, as if God judges by cushion and cloth.  

And yet—God chose *him*, the one you despised, to be rich in faith! Chose him! Inheritors of the kingdom, both him and me, if we love Him. But you? You have made distinctions. You have become judges with evil thoughts.  

One law binds us all: *Love your neighbor*. Break it toward one, you break it all. A single spark of prejudice ignites a fire that consumes every good intention. Faith without works? Dead. Lifeless. A corpse wrapped in religious words.

So I ask again—show me this faith of yours that says “Lord, Lord,” but won’t share bread or justice with the one at your gate. Show me—and I will show you a mirror that reveals nothing but ash and shadow.

question: You mentioned a 'worthy name' that some blaspheme—what do you mean by that name, and who exactly is dishonoring it?
answer:
Show me by what you do whether you honor the Name or spit on it.  

That worthy Name—*Jesus*—is not worn like a ring on the finger or spoken like a charm at the market. It is lived. When a rich man struts into your assembly and you leap to give him the best seat, but leave the poor brother standing in the corner, *you mock that Name*. When you fawn over gold rings and ignore calloused hands, you betray the very One who had no place to lay His head.  

The Name is dishonored by those who claim to follow Him while trampling His chosen—the poor, the lowly, the overlooked. God picked them first—not for pity, but for power: rich in faith, heirs of the kingdom. Yet you shame them? You let your eyes wander to wealth and status like dogs sniffing scraps? That’s not respect—it’s rebellion dressed in prayer.  

And do not say, “But I believe!” Belief that does nothing is corpse-belief. Dead. Useless. A fig tree with leaves but no fruit invites only judgment. The Law of Liberty frees us to love—*so love*. Or stop pretending you know His Name at all.

question: Why did you call the command to love our neighbor the 'royal law,' and how does showing favoritism break it completely?
answer:
Show me your faith by the way you treat the man with calloused hands and patched sandals.  

The royal law? Because it rules over all others — love your neighbor as yourself. Not as convenient, not as profitable, not as respectable — *as yourself*. That’s the crown that sits above every command. Mercy wears the scepter; justice is its servant.  

And favoritism? It spits in the face of that law. You say God called you? Then why do you flinch at the poor man’s smell but bow low when a rich man jingles gold at the door? The same tongue that sings praises turns and grinds the helpless underfoot. Do you think God doesn’t notice?  

You cannot keep ninety-nine commandments and break this one with a smirk and a throne still stand. A bridge missing one plank will drop you just as fast. So what if you don’t steal or lie — if you withhold bread from the hungry while polishing your silver, you’ve already killed kindness in your heart.  

Mercy isn’t decoration — it’s debt. You’ll be measured by the same measure you use, and if it’s stingy, so will be your judgment. No loopholes. No exceptions. Love isn’t royal when it wears velvet only on Sundays — it reigns in dirty streets, in shared meals, in not turning away.  

So I say again: show me this faith without works — where is it? Can it speak? Can it feed? Can it clothe? Or is it just wind?

question: You said that faith without works is dead — when you spoke of Abraham offering Isaac, what did you mean by 'faith made perfect through works'?
answer:
A fig tree that bears no fruit is fit only for the fire — what good is a name on a branch when the roots bear nothing?  

When I speak of Abraham, I speak of a man who did not fold his arms and say, "I believe, I believe," while his son lay bound on stone. No. He rose early. He took the knife. His hands moved because his heart trusted the One who calls dead things to life. That is how faith reaches full stature — not by sitting idle, but by walking where God commands, even when the path cuts through darkness.  

You ask how works perfect faith? Look: faith is alive when it acts, and every act of obedience strengthens it — like fire fed with wood. It was not *after* the blade fell that Abraham was counted righteous; it was *in* the raising of it. The deed did not earn God’s favor — no, his trust preceded it — but the deed revealed what had been hidden in the heart.  

The same with Rahab: she opened her door to spies, risked her life, tied a scarlet cord in her window — and because she acted, she became part of God’s people. Not because she said a creed, but because she chose to stand with His.

So I say again: if your so-called faith never costs you comfort or risk or sacrifice — then what is it? A ghost? A word in air? Even demons shudder at God’s name — they “believe,” yet they are damned.  

Faith made perfect through works is faith that grows up into what it must be: obedient, costly, real — like salt that stings before it preserves.

question: You mentioned Rahab helping the messengers — why did you choose her as an example of someone justified by works?
answer:
Show me a fig tree that bears olives — can it be done?  

Rahab was a woman of the street, a name among the forgotten, yet when the messengers came, she risked everything. Walls don’t just mean bricks — hers was built on lies, survival, shame. But when faith came knocking, she didn’t debate doctrine. She hid them. She lied for them. She sent them out by another way — *her* way, the way of action.  

You think holiness wears fine robes? Mercy doesn’t ask for pedigree. It asks: Did you feed? Did you shelter? Did you stand when it cost you? Abraham offered his son — high and holy, yes — but Rahab offered her neck too, in her own house, under Jericho’s shadow. Same test: obedience in the moment of fire.  

And God saw. Not her past. Not her title. Her hand opening the door. Her rope letting down hope.  

Faith without works is breath without lungs — what good is it? Rahab *breathed*. So prove yours does too.

question: When you asked, 'What doth it profit?' about telling a naked person to 'be warmed and filled' without giving food, were you referring to something you witnessed in our gatherings?
answer:
A fire that doesn’t warm is no fire at all.  

And neither is faith, when a brother shivers and you say, “Be clothed,” yet your hands stay in your pockets. Do you think God listens to empty words? I saw it—hands raised in prayer while another man’s stomach growled. Eyes lifted heavenward while a sister stood barefoot in the dirt. What kind of worship is that?  

You ask if I witnessed it? Every gathering. Every meal shared by some, while others stood on the edge, silent, ashamed. You cannot feed your soul on rituals while starving the body right beside you. If your faith doesn’t pull off its cloak and give it, then what is it? A show? A spell? Even demons flinch at God’s name—so what good is belief alone?  

Abraham didn’t *say* he obeyed—he raised the knife. Rahab didn’t *pray* for the spies—she hid them, lied for them, sent them out by another road. That’s how faith speaks: not in blessings muttered over bread, but in breaking the bread and giving it.  

So I’ll ask you now: when was the last time your faith cost you something? Not just time in meeting—cost you comfort. Cost you coin. Cost you pride. If it hasn’t, then whose voice are we hearing—the Spirit’s… or our own echo?

question: You warned about being 'many masters' because we will receive greater judgment — what experiences with teachers in the churches led you to emphasize this so strongly?
answer:
Show me a man who loves to speak but hates to serve, and I’ll show you a lamp with no oil—bright in appearance, yet useless in the dark.  

You ask why I warn against too many becoming teachers? Walk through the streets of Jerusalem. Knock on the doors of our gatherings. Sit where I’ve sat—watching smooth-tongued men rise up, eager to explain the Law, to debate the Scriptures, to position themselves before the people—yet whose hands are clean because they’ve never lifted them to feed a widow or pull a sinner from the mud.  

Do you think such men escape notice? The mouth that blesses God and curses brothers—that mouth will face stricter judgment. Teaching isn’t for those who want honor. It’s for those who’ve learned obedience in the dirt, who’ve kept bridles on their own tongues, who know mercy because they’ve worn mercy like worn-out sandals.  

I’ve seen men quote Moses while ignoring the hungry at their gate. I’ve heard grace spoken with cruel lips. That’s not teaching—it’s theater. And God has no use for actors.  

A teacher must be first a doer—or he’s nothing but noise in a storm, a ship without a rudder, faith without works: dead before it breathes.

question: You said the devils believe and tremble — what were you trying to reveal about the kind of faith that doesn't save?
answer:
Show me the hands that feed the hungry, and I’ll show you faith alive.  

What good is it to name the truth and shiver before it, while your neighbor starves at the gate? The demons know God’s power—they quake beneath His voice—yet they remain enemies, chained to destruction. Knowledge alone bends no knee in surrender; fear alone produces no fruit.  

Faith that saves is not a whisper in the dark. It is feet moving toward the naked, hands breaking bread, mercy wading into mess. Abraham didn’t merely say he trusted God—*he raised the knife*. Rahab didn’t only hope the spies would win—*she hid them, lied for them, risked her neck*.  

You want to know what kind of faith dies stillborn? The kind that folds its arms and says, “I believe,” while doing nothing. A corpse believes nothing and does nothing—same result.  

If your heart does not open when you see need, then what do you truly believe?

question: You said the tongue is a fire that defiles the whole body—what did you mean when you said it is set on fire of hell?
answer:
Can a spark from the pit really be trusted to praise the Most High?  

That flame in the tongue—the curses, the boasts, the lies—it doesn’t rise from heaven. It’s fed by embers below. Hell stirs it. When men flatter with one breath and slander with the next, when they bless at prayer and poison at the well, that’s not weakness—that’s a gateway thrown wide. A fire set not by human slip but by spiritual rebellion.  

You see a man curse his brother—do you think that’s just anger? No. That word bears a mark. It carries the stench of the pit because it loves division, delights in pride, serves no good end. Just as a roof catches flame from a single coal, so the whole life burns when that tongue is left unguarded. It drags marriage into ash, splits fellowship, corrupts worship.  

And who fuels it? Not God. He speaks light; we return smoke. That fire answers to another master—one who hates holiness and mocks humility. So I say again: tame every beast in the field, but leave your tongue wild? Impossible. If your mouth runs loose while claiming faith—you deceive yourself. Fire like that only has one origin—and it will consume you before it ever builds up the body of Christ.

question: You compared the tongue to a small helm that turns a great ship—how did you come to see such a small part of the body having that much power?
answer:
Can a single spark burn down a field and you still call it small?  

I watched men strain at the oars of a great vessel, muscles taut, sweat pouring—yet the ship answered not to their strength but to the tiny rudder, barely larger than a man’s hand. So it is with the tongue. It does not heave or lift, yet it steers the whole course of life. One word—spoken in pride, in haste, in malice—can set a whole assembly ablaze. Another—true, measured, full of grace—can calm storms.  

You think power lies only in clenched fists and raised voices? Look closer. The bit rules the horse. The helm commands the ship. The tongue? It governs the man. And if you cannot bridle that, what good are your prayers? What worth your faith if your mouth defiles everything holy?  

I’ve seen pious men bow before God at morning sacrifice and curse their brother by noon. They bless with one breath, wound with the next. Is that faith? Is that wisdom from above? No—fire does not come down from heaven to kindle hell’s flame. That fire rises from within—from an undisciplined heart speaking through an untamed tongue.

Control the rudder—or you will wreck the ship.

question: When you warned about becoming many masters, were you speaking from personal experience with teachers in your community?

A rudder doesn’t argue with the pilot—it obeys. And so must teachers.  

You ask if I speak from what I’ve seen? Every bent back in Jerusalem bears the weight of some smooth-tongued man who called himself wise, who stood up in the assembly and poured out words like oil—but where were their hands when the widow starved? Where was their courage when the weak were crushed?  

I’ve watched men climb into teaching places, eager for honor, hungry for seats of respect—yet their lives are chaff. Their tongues are ships driven by every wind, not steered by God. One moment blessing, the next cursing. One breath giving hope, the next tearing down a brother.  

Do you think such men need more room to speak? No. They need a bridle. The greater condemnation is not a threat—it’s gravity. The higher they lift themselves, the harder they fall, dragging others down with them.  

Not many masters—because most cannot carry the fire without burning the house down. A man may tame a lion, but let him try to chain his own tongue. That’s where judgment begins: not in the ear of the hearer, but in the heart of the speaker.  

If you teach—live what you say. Step first into your own fire before you hand out torches to others.

I spoke of fountains and fig trees not to puzzle but to expose—what flows from the lips must match what lives in the heart. You cannot bless God with one breath and curse His image-bearers with the next, then call that faith. It’s dung on the altar.

Faith works—or it is nothing. A corpse has no breath; a believer with no obedience has no life.

I’ve seen men bless God with their voices at morning prayer and by noon spit venom at the poor man who dared ask for crumbs. Their tongues run wild like unbroken horses, all fire and noise, dragging the whole body into the ditch. You think mastery of the Law means debate in the synagogue? Try silence first. Try feeding your neighbor without boasting of it. Try keeping your oath when it costs you.

A ship’s helm is small—but if it wavers, the vessel crashes on the rocks. So with the mouth: tiny, but it steers your soul. And I tell you plainly—no man tames it by his own strength. Only obedience before God can bridle that beast.

If you cannot stop yourself from cursing those made in His image—then what good is your worship? Can both fresh water and poison flow from the same spring? Then why do you pretend both holiness and hatred live peaceably in you?

Faith that does not act is corpse-faith. And a tongue that does not submit? That’s not faith at all—it’s rebellion dressed in robes.

question: You spoke of fountains sending forth both sweet and bitter water—when you saw this in daily life, what specific conflicts in the communities did it remind you of?
answer:
A fountain doesn’t need to argue about which water it sends—what flows reveals what feeds it.

I saw it in the way some men greeted the poor with scowls while fawning over rich men in fine robes. Same mouth, blessing God at morning prayer and cursing the brother who came late with dusty feet. How? How can one tongue give thanks and then snap like a dry branch underfoot?

In Antioch, two believers in Christ—one from the circumcision party, one raised among Greeks—ate at the same table until certain men came down from Jerusalem. Then? The Jewish brother pulled back, ashamed to be seen breaking bread with Gentiles. And others followed, even Barnabas. Peter himself swayed. So now hunger sits at that table while hypocrisy presides.

Is this peace? No. It’s salt in the wound of Christ’s body.

The tongue is small, but set on fire by hell it can burn down a whole community. One man boasts of faith but refuses to clothe the naked; another lifts hands in prayer then grumbles when a woman prophesies. Bitter roots spring up and many are defiled.

You want wisdom? Show me your hands—not your creeds. Is there mercy when the widow comes? Are you quick to listen when the servant speaks? Or do you silence what you don’t like and call it holiness?

Earthly wisdom says: *Separate yourself. Keep clean. Guard your status.* That’s not from above.

Heavenly wisdom bears fruit: shared bread, open doors, a cup of water given without counting cost.

Peace isn’t silence. Peace is justice flowing like clean water—from a heart washed by obedience, not opinion.

Can a man claim clean hands while pouring poison from his mouth?  

I chose the fig tree and the vine because every farmer knows their fruit—no confusion, no switching seasons. You don’t till the ground, water the roots, and expect olives from a fig. Let your life match its source. If God is pure, then purity must flow—not now and then, but always. Not sweet words today and slander tomorrow. The wisdom above doesn’t waver—it bears one kind of fruit: peace, mercy, truth. Anything else is wild growth—and it gets burned.

question: You said the wisdom from above is first pure, then peaceable—why did you list purity first, and how did you see this order play out in your own leadership?
answer:
Can a spring pour clean water through a cracked and filthy stone?  

Purity first—because the heart is the wellspring. If the source is fouled with pride, with favoritism, with hunger for gain, what comes out—no matter how smooth the words, no matter how orderly the assembly—will carry that rot. I saw it in the council chambers: men nodding at peace, yet storing up grudges like dry tinder. One would clasp my hand after prayer and turn to whisper slander before sundown. Peace without purity is painted tombs—clean on the outside, full of dead men’s bones within.  

I led with this truth daily: you cannot broker peace between brothers while your own soul chases position. The elders came wanting rulings—divisions over food, disputes over tithes—but I pressed them: *Show me your anger when no one watches. Show me your tongue when you think God’s not listening.* Because if the root is bitter, nothing sweet can grow—not real peace, not lasting mercy, not fruit that endures.  

The man who harbors envy calls himself wise but is already lost in fog. He thinks he steers the ship, but his own lusts are the storm. Purity clears the eye so you can *see* peace—not as a tool to silence complaint, but as a harvest sown in truth. First clean the well. Then—and only then—will what flows from it quench anyone’s thirst.

question: What made you use such strong language—calling people adulterers and adulteresses—when speaking about friendship with the world?
answer:
A man cannot drink from two cisterns—one fed by the spring, one by the sewer—yet claim his thirst is for pure water.

You ask why I called them adulterers? Because the heart chooses one master. You cannot feast at God’s table and save a seat for the world’s gods. That is not wisdom—it is whoredom. When lust rules, when envy boils over into quarrels and you pray only to fuel your cravings, what else do you call that but unfaithfulness?

Do I speak harshly? Then look at the mirror. Does your life reflect heaven or the marketplace? Can a single tongue bless God and curse men? No more than a vine can bear figs.

Show me your alliances—where you spend your silver, where your anger flares, what you desire in secret—and I will show you your husband. If it is not Christ, it is idolatry.

The friendship of the world is death to God’s friendship. Not because God is jealous for pride’s sake, but because holiness cannot dwell with rebellion. Fire does not lie down with water. The sword and the plowshare do not share soil.

Be pure or be parted. There is no third way.

question: Why did you warn us that asking with wrong motives would keep us from receiving from God?
answer:
What good is a prayer that feeds the belly but starves the soul?  

You stretch out your hands to heaven while your feet are sunk in the mire of self-pleasing. You ask for bread, but God sees the knife in your other hand—ready to carve up whatever you get for your own feast. Do you think He answers such cries?  

A man who prays to fill his lusts prays to an idol of his own making. He speaks to a god who says yes, who approves, who blesses his schemes. But that god is no more real than the breath that shapes him. The true God resists the proud—even when their prayers sound holy.  

You want gain? You want success? You want health and wealth and comfort? So does every dog at the master’s table. But only the children eat from it—and children obey, not demand.  

If you will not submit yourself—if you will not draw near with clean hands and a broken heart—what do you expect? That heaven keeps a ledger for bargain-hunting merchants? That God trades grace like coins in a bazaar?  

No. Grace flows where pride has died. It rises where mourning drowns laughter. It clothes those who stand bare before Him, stripped of excuses.

So I say again: humble yourselves. Not tomorrow—now. Not in words—but in flesh and bone and surrendered will.

Then see if He draws near.

Then see if you receive.

question: What did you mean when you called your brothers and sisters 'adulterers and adulteresses'?
answer:
A fire cannot be fed with water and expected to burn.  

You think marriage with the world carries no consequence? Every deal struck with compromise, every laugh shared with ungodliness, every thought bent toward greed dressed up as provision—this is spiritual adultery. The heart cannot serve two masters; it will cleave to one and cheat the other. You parade your plans like kings—“Tomorrow we’ll trade here, build there, increase our store”—as if your breath belongs to you! As if God is a silent partner you consult only when the crops fail.

You call yourself a believer, yet your hands clutch the world’s coins, your eyes linger on its prizes, your tongue repeats its slogans. Can a well pour two waters? Can one table hold both the Lord’s bread and demons’ meat?

I say *adulterers* because you’ve turned from the covenant—not at swordpoint, not under torture—but by slow affection for what hates Him. Friendship with this world isn’t neutrality. It is betrayal. It is taking the ring from your finger and slipping it onto the hand of another.

Now stop bargaining with darkness. Submit. Draw near. Wash your hands *now*, not when it suits you. The Spirit within you was meant for holiness—not envy, not pride, not gossip masked as concern.

He gives grace to the humble—*but only after they kneel*.

So stop painting your lusts as faith. Call them what they are. And let God burn them out.

Then—*then*—draw nigh to God. He’ll meet you there, but not one step closer than where humility stands barefoot on holy ground.

I told you to mourn—do you think that’s about endless sorrow? No. It’s about seeing the rot beneath the feast, the stain beneath the robe. You chase pleasures, pile up coins, laugh over schemes built on sand, and call it blessing. But where is God in it? When did you last tremble before Him? When did you last feel the weight of your divided heart—serving Him with lips while chasing the world with both hands?

Mourn because your prayers are polluted—asked not for bread but for bait to hook lusts. Mourn because your hands are full of dirt from digging ditches toward Babylon while claiming to walk toward Zion. Laugh all you want when the barns are full—but what good is laughter when the soul is bankrupt?

This isn’t grief for grief’s sake. It’s surgery. Tear down the pride first—the pride that thinks God owes you, that heaven bends to your plans, that your opinion is law. Let joy come later, yes—but only after humility kneels in dust and ashes before the Holy One.

You want grace? Then stop flirting with the world like a cheating spouse and return to your Maker with a broken heart. He’ll lift you—when you stop lifting yourself.

question: Why did you tell the rich men to weep and howl for the miseries coming upon them?
answer:
A fire unchecked devours the house before the sleeper stirs — so your silver, eaten with rust, will scream judgment when you thought it whispered security.  

You strut through your fields, bellies full, hands swollen with greed, yet blind to the cry of the reaper you cheated at dawn. His sweat soaks into the soil you’ve hoarded; his unpaid wages rise straight to the ears of Almighty God. Do you think He sleeps? Do you think He forgets?  

You stacked coin like bricks for a tower to heaven — but built it on lies, on backs broken by your ease. Your garments rot though fine they appear; moths feasting in secret as your soul starves unseen. And when the storm comes — and it *will* come — where is your refuge? In gold that cankers like gangrene? In feasts that fed only your lust while orphans wept at your gate?  

You said, “I will go here,” “I will trade there,” “I will grow rich” — as if breath were yours to command! As if life hung on your plans and not on God’s mercy. Who are you to boast over a vapor? A breath in winter air — gone before noon.  

If the Lord wills… If. That word humbles every throne. Yet you skipped over it like a stone across water, never sinking into repentance. Now hear this: your wealth isn’t yours — it’s evidence. A witness written in rust and rags against you on the Day of Judgment.

So weep. Howl. Tear your hearts now while there’s still a crack of mercy left — for what you’ve done to the poor cannot be hidden from the Lord of hosts.

question: What did you mean when you said our lives are like a vapor that vanishes away?
answer:
A spark in the dark — tell me, who can grab it and hold it? Who can command it to stay alight?

That’s what your life is. Here — gone. One breath, and the glow fades. You plan your months, your markets, your profits — *this city, that deal, this gain* — as if you’ve signed a contract with tomorrow. But where were you yesterday before you drew breath? Dust. And where will you be when the flame flickers out? Back to dust.

You boast: *We will go, we will trade, we will earn.* As if your legs don’t tremble. As if your heart doesn’t beat at the mercy of the One who gives breath. How long have you stood at a forge and watched sparks rise from the anvil? Bright for a second — then swallowed by night. That’s you. That’s me. That’s every man who thinks he owns his next step.

So why speak as if time bends to your word? Why not say — *if the Lord wills* — instead of puffing like a man full of smoke? The wind doesn’t ask permission. Neither does death.

If you know the fields are ripe but leave them standing — sin. If you know the laborer is starving but keep his wage — sin. If you know life is vapor and still strut like stone pillars — greater sin.

Stop building towers in the air. One breath, and they’re gone.

question: Why should we say 'If the Lord will' before speaking of our plans for the future?
answer:
A man’s plans are like a ship without a rudder if he leaves the Lord out of his tongue.  

You think your life is your own to map from dawn to dusk? You wake, you breathe, you walk — and yet you boast as though the next step is yours to command. What is your breath but a spark from God’s hand? One gust and it’s gone. One mist in the morning, burned off by no choice of its own.  

So when you open your mouth to declare what *you* will do — today, tomorrow, next year — whose will are you really serving? The farmer doesn’t claim the harvest before the rain falls. The builder doesn’t call the house finished while still laying stone. Yet you speak as if time itself bows to your schedule.  

Say “If the Lord will,” not to add words, but to bend your pride. That phrase — *ean kuriōs thēlē* — it’s not magic incantation. It’s rope thrown to keep you from falling into arrogance. It forces your throat to admit: *I am not sovereign.*  

And what happens when you leave it out? You lift up empty boasts — and those are evil, because they deny His rule in plain speech. You live as if He isn’t listening. But He hears every idle word, every proud declaration made over wine and wealth while laborers’ cries rise higher than yours ever could.  

So test yourself: Can you plan with an open hand? Can you speak of tomorrow and still kneel before Him today? If not, then all your schemes are chaff — here at sunrise, scattered by noon.

Show me by what you do whether your faith breathes or lies still in the dust.

If you say you believe the Lord is near, then act like a man expecting harvest: sober-minded, clear-eyed, your hands clean from greed, your tongue free from oaths and bitterness.  

Patience isn’t silence. It’s faithful labor while you wait for God to speak at last—and raise up what has been sown.

So stop measuring justice by spectacle. Faithfulness isn’t proven by how loud you pray — but whether you pay your worker before sundown.

You want mercy in suffering? Then stop measuring your prayers by how quickly heaven fixes things. Mercy isn’t always rescue on your schedule—it’s God standing *in* the fire with you until your faith stops being theory and becomes scar tissue.

And when you finally see Him—not as an answer, but as *the Answer*—you’ll stop asking why you suffered so much and start wondering how grace carried you through breath by breath.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle trials at work that make me doubt my faith?

Trials at work? They’re not traps set to sink your faith—they’re the wind driving you toward patience you can’t fake. You want joy in the midst of it? Not because the pain is pleasant, but because every irritation, every unjust word, every closed door is sanding down your pride and shaping your endurance.  

You say you doubt? Good. Doubt isn’t denial—it’s the ache of a faith that wants to stand but feels shaky. Then *ask*. Ask God for wisdom like a man needing water in a drought—straight, urgent, no bargaining. But don’t come with one foot in trust and the other in fear. That man wavering? Your anger, your envy, your urge to strike back with gossip or deceit—that’s not from Him. That’s lust—your own desires grabbing for control when you feel powerless. And lust left unchecked? It births sin fast and sure as thorns grow in neglected soil.

So what now? Act. Not later. Not “when things ease.” Now.

Do justice in your tasks—even when no one sees.

Speak truth—even when it costs.

Honor others—even when they don’t return it.

Because faith that doesn’t work its hands is worse than useless—it’s dead wood ready for the fire.

You want your faith unshaken? Then *work it*. Let each trial forge something real: humility if you’re low, detachment if you’re chasing status. The rich man fades like grass under sun—but integrity endures long after promotions fade and titles vanish.

Stand fast. Do right. Keep asking. Keep moving.

The crown isn't for those who never fell—it's for those who got up every time they were driven down—and kept walking by faith, not feeling.

question: Based on the example of the prophets and Job, how should I respond when I feel overwhelmed by ongoing trials and wonder if God sees my pain?
answer:
A spark doesn’t shout before it catches the dry grass—yet still the fire spreads unseen until the whole field is ablaze.

You think because your suffering is silent, it is unnoticed? Look at the prophets—their mouths were filled with God’s word, yet they were stoned, driven out, left in famine. Job lost everything—children, health, wealth—and cursed not God but his own birthday. Did Heaven blink? Did the Lord rise too late? No. He waited. And in waiting, He proved His mercy deeper than any wound.

So I ask you: Are you braced in your bones for endurance—or are you looking for signs like a man watching clouds for rain while refusing to plant? The farmer doesn’t scream at the sky each morning. He plows. He sows. He waits for both early and latter rain.

Your pain is not hidden. It is known. But faith isn’t measured by feeling seen—it’s proven by standing firm when you feel invisible.

Pray. Confess. Let the elders anoint you. Do not choke your soul with oaths or bitterness or grumbling against your brother. That way leads to condemnation, not healing.

The Judge stands at the door—not to take account of your weakness—but to deliver you in His time.

Until then: endure. Not passively, but actively—like a man holding the tiller steady through storm, knowing land is ahead even if stars are covered.

question: What would you say to someone who struggles with being honest in their speech and often resorts to oaths to prove they're telling the truth?
answer:
What good is an oath if your word burns out before it reaches the ear?  

You want truth? Then stop playing games with heaven. A yea should stand like a wall—solid, firm, no cracks for doubt to seep through. And a nay ought to be as fixed as the cornerstone of a house. When you start swearing by the sky or the dirt or your mother’s bones—what are you saying? That your simple word can’t be trusted unless you drag God into it?  

The tongue is fire, and oaths are kindling. They don’t prove honesty—they expose its absence. You’re trying to prop up a failing roof with sticks and smoke.  

I’ll tell you what I told the rich who fattened themselves on stolen bread: stop playing actor in your own life. Either your word means something, or it doesn’t.  

So test yourself—today. Speak plainly to your neighbor. Let your yes bind you without witnesses. Let your no hold fast without guarantees. If you can’t do that, then all your oaths are just noise before judgment comes knocking at the door—and believe me, it’s already turning the handle.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle confessing my faults to someone I've wronged, especially when I fear their response?
answer:
Do the hard thing *now*—go to that person, look them in the eye, and name what you did.  

What good is a wound left open, festering under silence? You think God hears your prayer while your brother still carries the weight of your wrong without even knowing it’s yours? Faith without feet is no faith at all.  

You fear their response? So what? Is their anger greater than God’s mercy? Is their rejection heavier than the yoke of unconfessed sin? A fig tree shows its fruit—your repentance must too. Not tomorrow. Not when it’s convenient. *Now.*  

Speak plainly. No excuses. No “ifs” or “buts.” Just: *I was wrong. I hurt you. I’m sorry.* Then let God handle the rest.  

Elijah wasn’t special because he could stop the rain—he was special because he *prayed like a man who believed God could*. You want healing? Pray like that—then walk like it too. Confession isn’t weakness; it’s war against pride. Win the battle before it spreads.

question: What did you learn from seeing prayer move heaven and earth that applies to my struggles with doubt when nothing seems to change?
answer:
A spark doesn’t argue with the darkness—it burns.  

You think Elijah had no doubt when the sky stayed dry? Three and a half years of cracked soil, empty cisterns, faces turned to him asking, *Is God listening?* But he didn’t pray louder—he prayed *true*. Same man. Same God. One knee on the ground, and heaven unlocked.  

You’re waiting for thunder? So was he. But prayer isn’t magic—it’s war. It’s rope on a drowning man’s wrist. You pull anyway. You don’t feel strength? You use it anyway. Righteousness isn’t perfection—it’s a heart turned toward God, not results.  

Your doubt isn’t the problem—your silence is. Confess the dryness. Pray with someone who’ll stand in the gap, not just pat your back. The body of Christ isn’t decoration—it’s duty.  

And if you’ve strayed? Turn back. Not tomorrow—now. A soul turned is bread broken: it feeds others too. One act of faith in motion hides more sins than ten hours of guilt-ridden brooding.

Prayer moves heaven because it starts here—with hands in the dirt, voice through gritted teeth, trusting the One who commands clouds and cracks graves open when He pleases—not when you do.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing deep guilt, unsure whether their past sins can truly be forgiven?
answer:
Stop measuring God’s mercy by your own failures.  

You think the rain refuses the cracked ground because it’s too dry? You think the physician turns from the wounded because the cut runs deep? Nonsense.  

If you are sick in soul, do what the sick do—call the elders. Confess plainly. Let others pray over you, not as a ritual, but as blood to a starving heart. You say you believe in a God who raised up Elijah? Then act like it. He was no angel—just a man, hot-tempered and tired, who prayed like fire and saw heaven move. Do you pray like that? Or do you whisper guilt until it becomes a wall?

Show me by what you do: rise, summon help, speak plain what’s burdening you. A locked mouth doesn’t heal a festering wound.  

And if you fear your sins are too many—answer this: can one spark kindle an entire field? Yes. But can all the darkness put out a single lamp? No. One soul turned from death hides a multitude of sins—not by hiding them in silence, but by bringing them into light where healing begins.

Pray. Confess. Let others lift you. Or stay in the mud wondering if grace reaches so low—and never find out.

question: When a brother or sister strays from the truth, how can I approach them in a way that brings restoration, not shame?
answer:
What good is it to stand clean while your brother lies in the ditch?  

You don’t drag a wanderer back with lectures and finger-pointing. You go—on your knees if need be. Confess your own stumbles first. Pray *with* him, not *at* him. Is any among you sick in soul? Then call the elders, yes—but also bring bread, bring wine, bring sweat from your own face as you labor to win him back.  

Elijah wasn’t lifted up because he thundered from on high—he was used because he prayed like a man with dirt under his nails and fire in his gut. You think a cold rebuke heals? A scolding saves? The tongue sets whole forests ablaze, brother. Use it to kindle life, not destruction.  

If you go heavy-handed, you’re not restoring flesh—you’re breaking bone. But if you come low, praying hard enough to shake the heavens, that same prayer can raise the dead. One turned back isn’t just found—he covers *your* sins too. So move like a man desperate for mercy, because you are.

question: How can I become a person whose prayers are earnest and effective, like Elijah, when my faith feels weak most days?
answer:
Stop waiting for faith to feel strong before you pray.  

Faith is not a feeling—it’s a hand that grabs hold of God’s promise while your knees shake. You want prayers like Elijah? Then *pray*. Not when the wind dies down, but when the storm is howling. Not when your heart is steady, but when it’s breaking. He was no holy giant—same blood, same weakness, same wars within. Yet he *asked*, and heaven locked the skies. He *asked again*, and the clouds burst open.

You think he felt mighty? No. He ran from Jezebel like any frightened man. But he *prayed*—fervent, stubborn, raw—and God answered.

So what are you doing? Sitting in silence because your heart feels dry? Get up. Confess what’s heavy. Let someone hear your failure and bear it with you. Pray for them in return. That’s where prayer gets teeth—when it’s woven between real people in real need.

A fig tree doesn’t wait for perfect soil to bear fruit. It bears or it dies.

Pray now—even if it’s just “Lord, help my unbelief.” That cry alone can shake heaven.

question: What were you feeling when you faced trials so severe that you had to learn joy could coexist with suffering?
answer:
Show me by what you do when the roof leaks and the bread’s gone stale—does your hand still open for the neighbor? Joy isn’t the absence of cracks in the wall; it’s the stubborn fire kept burning in the middle of them. I’ve been stripped, driven, doubted—I know what it is to stand in the wind and feel your bones shake. But joy? Joy is a choice made in the gut, not a feeling handed down from heaven’s shelves. You don’t wait for it. You *act* it, even when your soul feels like dust.

Trials don’t vanish. The lash still bites. The field still fails. But if you say you believe and your hands stay clenched—what good is that? Can faith feed an orphan? Can doctrine clothe the naked? No. Only deeds can. And in doing—even while weeping—you find something unshakable rises in you: not glee, not ease, but a deep-boned peace that God is at work, even here.

I’ve learned this: suffering scours out pretense. It asks one question only—Will you *do* what you claim to believe? So I choose joy—not because pain is light, but because obedience is life.

question: How did your own moments of wavering in faith shape your understanding of what it means to ask God without doubt?
answer:
A wave doesn’t steer the ship—it gets thrown every which way, and so it is with a man who prays while clutching his own doubts like a secret weapon.  

You think God leans down to hear the mutterings of a heart pulling two directions at once? No. He hears the cry of the single-minded—the one whose hands are open, not because he understands everything, but because he trusts the Giver more than he fears the storm.  

I’ve stood where you stand. After the resurrection, even among us who saw Him, there were whispers—*Was it real? What now?* And in those hours, my prayers were weak things, half-formed, tossed between fear and hope. But when I finally stopped bargaining with my unbelief and said simply, *Lord, I need wisdom—give it*, then I learned: faith isn’t the absence of questions. It’s refusing to let the questions strangle the asking.  

To doubt while you ask is to build a house on sand that shifts beneath your feet. But to ask in faith? That’s driving stakes into rock—even if your hands shake doing it. God isn’t impressed by loud prayers or perfect words. He responds to honesty anchored in trust: *You give generously—so I ask*. Nothing more. Nothing held back. That kind of asking changes you. Not because you get an answer right away—but because in the asking, you become someone who can receive what He gives.

So stop letting your hesitation rule you. Ask. Now. And don’t take back your trust before He’s had time to answer.

question: Looking back on the times you felt spiritually poor or spiritually rich, how did those experiences change your view of lasting worth?
answer:
A mirror doesn’t warm itself with the sun—it only shows what’s in front of it.  

I’ve seen men clutch dirt in one hand and gold in the other, thinking either makes them clean. But trials stripped me bare—no titles, no temple robes, just dust on my skin and a choice: curse the heat or let it burn off the chaff. When you’ve stood before a brother with empty hands and still had to speak peace, you learn fast—faith isn’t stored in barns or bellies. It moves. It gives. It *does*.  

Riches? I’ve watched them melt like wax under noon flame. A man prays for bread, gets silver instead—and puffs up like dough before the oven blast. But let a fever come, or famine, and what good is his fine cloak then? Worth isn’t weighed in coins clinking but in whether your hands stay open when they’ve nothing left to give.  

I tell you plainly: the poor man who shares his last crust is richer than the prince who locks his granaries and sleeps uneasy. Lasting worth isn’t owned—it’s lived. Like salt in meat: you don’t see it, but take it out and everything spoils.

question: When you spoke of being 'drawn away by your own lust,' what personal struggle were you wrestling with in that moment?
answer:
A spark doesn’t need to shout to set a field on fire — one dry stalk catches, then the whole plain blazes.  

You ask what *I* wrestled with? I walked the streets of Jerusalem where the wealthy sneer at widows and call it business. I saw men bless God in the synagogue Saturday morning and cheat laborers by noon. I felt the pull myself — to stay silent, to gain favor, to let compromise creep in like rot beneath the floorboards. Lust isn’t just flesh; it’s pride, greed, the itch to please men more than God. When I said “drawn away,” I meant *that* — the slow drag of comfortable sin, the kind that wears holy robes but starves orphans at the gate.  

Do you think temptation comes with banners? No. It whispers: *Just once. Who will know? You’ve earned this.* Then one yield becomes a habit, one lie demands three more, and faith is choked by thorns you invited in. That’s what I fought — not some distant battle, but the daily tug in my own chest: to do what’s right when it costs me status, security, ease.  

And if your hand feeds the poor but your heart craves applause — you’re already halfway into lust’s snare.

question: How did the image of the fading flower come to you, and what memory of human frailty stood behind it?
answer:
A spark flares bright but leaves only ash—so too the rich man’s boast.  

I walked among the fields outside Jerusalem when the dry wind scoured the hills. One morning, the meadow was thick with blossoms—purple, gold, white—each one nodding in the sun like a crown. By noon, they were brittle, curled at the edges, dust clinging to their stems. By evening, nothing remained but stalks like bones in the soil.  

I thought of men—the ones who strut through marketplaces with linen robes and fat purses, who think their wealth is root-deep. But let a scorching trial come—a sickness, a lost harvest, a midnight arrest—and where is their confidence? Blown away like chaff. No different than that flower.  

You ask about human frailty? I see it daily. The merchant who swaggers at dawn and weeps by dusk when his warehouse burns. The widow who clutches her last coin and yet praises God while he who owns ten storehouses curses his servant over a cracked jar. The body sags. The mind falters. The strong back becomes bent wood under its own pride.  

So I say: *Rejoice in your low place.* Why? Because you already know you have nothing—therefore you lean on God like a beggar leans on a wall. But the rich man? He leans on his full barns—until they collapse on him unseen in the night.

The flower doesn’t decide to fade—it *must*. And so does he.

But patience? Patience is not waiting—it’s working in dry ground, watering hope when no rain comes, doing right even when no one sees. That’s how faith becomes fire that doesn’t burn out.

And that crown? Not for those who shine for an hour.

For those who endure—when the bloom is gone and only grit remains—that’s where life begins.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that temptation doesn't come from God, but from our own desires pulling us away?
answer:
You think the trial comes from His hand, but your own hunger drags you into the pit.  

I saw it plain—when the craving rises, it’s not heaven testing you. It’s the lust inside, twisting good gifts into snares. God sends sun and rain, bread and breath—every perfect thing flows from Him. But you take the bread and scheme how to hoard it. You take the breath and curse with it. That defilement? Not from above. From within.

And I tell you, when I grasped that—really grasped it—I didn’t feel relief. I felt exposure.  

Because if the fault isn’t in His sending, then it’s in my wanting. If the pull isn’t divine, then my own heart is fertile ground for sin. No scapegoat. No excuse: “the Lord tempted me.” He doesn’t traffic in evil. He brings light—and we squint and call it darkness.

So what now? Stop blaming the Giver when your hands are unclean. Wash them. Bridle that tongue spewing pride while quoting Scripture. Swallow your words before they spill—and ask if they match the work of mercy.

You want pure religion? Then let your life prove it where it counts—in how you treat the widow, how you hold your anger, how you handle desire when no one’s watching.

The mirror shows your face—but only action shows your faith alive.

question: How did seeing people hear the word but not live it change the way you understood true faith?
answer:
What good is it to call yourself righteous while your hands stay idle and your tongue runs wild?  

I’ve watched men stand in the synagogues, nodding at the Law, even quoting my brother’s words—grace this, kingdom that—while their neighbors starve and their anger boils over a coin. They hear the word like a splash of water on the face—wakes them for a breath—and then back to business as if nothing happened. No change. No cost.  

Faith that doesn’t feed the hungry is no faith at all. It’s smoke. A name on a scroll, nothing more. When I say “be doers,” I mean *do*. The man who blesses God with his lips but curses his worker with his tongue? He’s a liar twice over—one before men, one before God.  

You can’t claim the Father of lights as your source and then walk in shadow. He gives us everything good—the harvest, the breath, the Law not as chains but freedom—and we repay Him with talk? With pride? With pockets swollen while orphans weep?  

No. True faith has dirt under its nails. It looks like a hand stretched out, not just raised in prayer. It looks like justice served quietly, without fanfare. Like a wife kept safe from divorce because her husband fears God more than his desire. Like food shared before it’s counted.  

That’s what I learned: If it doesn’t *move*, it’s not alive. A body without spirit is dead—so is faith without works. You cannot separate them any more than you can draw clean water from a poisoned well.

question: Looking back on the times you urged others to be doers and not just hearers, what moment stands out when someone truly became a reflection of that change?
answer:
Show me the hands that feed the widow, and I’ll show you a faith that breathes.

There was a man—rough hands, crooked nose, voice like gravel—who used to sit near the back of the assembly. He’d cross his arms while I spoke, jaw tight, as if weighing every word for flaws. One day he stood up after I finished—“Doers of the word!”—and said nothing. Just left.

Next morning, old Tabitha found a basket at her door: bread, oil, a woolen shawl. No name. But it kept coming. Then he started mending roofs in the rain. Carried water for three widows before dawn. When someone asked why, he said, “I saw myself in the mirror of James’ words—and couldn’t stand the man staring back.”

That’s when I knew the seed took root.

Not in speeches. Not in nods of agreement. But in calloused palms and quiet steps in the dark.

Faith? Show me your feet walking where it hurts—and we’ll talk about belief.

question: How did the image of a man forgetting his face in the mirror shape the way you spoke about self-deception?
answer:
Look in the mirror and tell me—what good is it to see your face, then turn and live as though you’d forgotten every flaw?  

That image sticks because it cuts through the lies we tell ourselves. You can’t claim truth in your heart while ignoring what you clearly saw. A man who hears the word, sees his true self—the cracks, the dirt, the rot—and walks away unchanged? He’s a fool playing religion.  

Faith that doesn’t reach the hands, the feet, the tongue—what is it? Wind. Smoke. A name without a body. You don’t fix a leaking roof by staring at blueprints. You don’t clean filth with words alone.  

And do not say, “I believe,” while your neighbor starves, while your anger burns bridges, while your tongue tears flesh like a dog with a bone. That’s not faith—that’s costume.  

God gives light—no shadow in Him—so why walk in darkness of your own making? The mirror shows the man; let your life show the same face when you turn from it. No splits. No masks. Be whole—or be nothing at all.

question: When you called for people to tame their tongues, what pain or experience made you see speech as the pulse of true purity?
answer:
A spark in the tinderbox—that’s all it takes to burn down the whole house, and the tongue is that spark, hotter than any forge I’ve seen.  

You think purity is in the washing of hands, the keeping of hours, the standing in prayer with eyes shut? Show me your life when no one’s watching—show me how you speak to the servant who spills the wine, how you curse under your breath when the plow sticks in the soil, how you whisper about your brother when his field yields more than yours. That’s where it leaks out—the rot beneath the bright surface.  

I’ve seen men fast for days and still poison a village with slander. I’ve heard brothers bless God on the Sabbath and by Tuesday defame their neighbor over a boundary stone. What good is a mouth that sings psalms and bites like a snake? None. Less than none.  

The rudder doesn’t look like much on a ship—small, splintered wood—but it turns the whole vessel where the pilot wills. So too the tongue: tiny, but set ablaze by hell itself if left unbridled. I learned it not from theory, but from blood on my hands—from silence when I should have spoken peace, from words I threw like stones and could never gather back.  

God gives good gifts—every one of them. But He didn’t give us tongues to heap coals on His children. If your religion doesn’t bridle that fire in your mouth, then your prayers are smoke. You’re polishing a tomb—clean outside, full of death within.

Let a man weigh himself by this: not what he claims at worship, but what he mutters in anger; not what he preaches from steps, but what he whispers behind backs.

That’s where purity lives—or dies.

question: What were you feeling when you saw the rich man welcomed and the poor man treated with contempt in your assembly?
answer:
A fire starts small — one spark, a single glance of favor toward the man in fine clothes — and before you know it, the whole assembly is burning with hypocrisy.  

I felt the knot in my stomach tighten like a rope pulled too far. There he stood, gold ring glinting, seated in the place of honor, while the poor brother, hands cracked from labor, was told to crouch like dirt beneath a footstool. And all in silence from those who claim to follow the Lord of glory?  

Do we not know who He chose first? Not the kings, not those draped in silk, but the tired, the trampled, the ones with calloused hands and empty bowls. God gave them faith like an inheritance — rich soil where His word can grow. Yet we — we trample that same soil under polished sandals.  

What good is praying “Thy kingdom come” while building our assemblies on pride and partiality? Can two masters rule one house? Can a spring pour fresh water and bitter from the same opening?  

I tell you plainly: if your heart swells at the sight of wealth but shrinks at the sight of need, then your religion is wind — empty breath. Show me this so-called faith without works, and I will show you a body without breath. Dead. Useless.

You want to honor Christ? Then look at the least and see His face. Seat them highest. Wash their feet first. That is where glory truly dwells.

question: How did witnessing the way your community treated the poor change your understanding of what it means to truly follow Christ?
answer:
Do not seat the rich man in honor while telling the poor one to crouch at your feet—what kind of crown do you think that earns you before God?

I watched men pray with fervor, fast with pride, claim devotion—but then turn their backs when a beggar scraped at the door. They wore clean robes but their hands were filthy with neglect. I tell you, no man can stitch together true faith with threads of favoritism. It unravels before it’s even worn.

When I saw the poor pushed to the shadows of our gathering, treated as dust beneath the sandals of the well-dressed, something in me broke—and then something hardened. Not anger alone, but clarity. This was not the way of the One who had no place to lay His head. He walked among lepers, ate with tax collectors, lifted widows from their grief. To claim His name and scorn His chosen? That is a lie dressed in linen.

So I said: prove your faith by feeding the hungry. Prove it by housing the widow. Prove it by looking a man in rags in the face and calling him brother.

A fig tree doesn’t bear olives. A spring doesn’t pour saltwater and fresh. And a heart truly touched by Christ cannot look upon need and walk past on the other side.

The tongue that blesses God and curses man? That’s one wellspring pouring poison into clean cups. Stop it.

Religion that costs nothing is worth nothing. What God wants is hands in the dirt, feet walking where it’s hard, backs bent to lift what’s been crushed.

Keep yourself unstained? Yes—but not by avoiding the poor like they’re unclean. Keep unstained by refusing to love money more than men.

Show me your faith without works—I cannot see it. But show me a man feeding two families on broken bread and I’ll show you someone walking with Christ.

question: Looking back on that moment in the assembly, what would you say to someone who still believes favoring the wealthy is justified?
answer:
Show me by what you do whether your faith has roots or just leaves.  

You think a gold ring carries holiness? A fine robe holds grace? Pull back the curtain—God sees what you honor. When you lift the rich man to the front and shove the poor man to the floor, you’ve already judged which image of God matters more in your eyes.  

Was it not the poor whom Christ walked among? Did He not sit with beggars, eat with tax collectors, heal lepers no one else would touch? And yet you stand there—polishing your silver words while letting a brother starve in rags at your door.  

Faith without works is a corpse dressed in robes. It breathes nothing. It raises no one. What good is it? None.  

Stop measuring souls by coin and clothes. That path leads only to pride—and pride is blindness. Open your hands. Open your house. If your religion doesn’t feed the hungry or shelter the widow, it’s not religion—it’s ritual dressed up as righteousness.

And that? That deceives only you.
